coredata.dc:title: Kinetic modeling of ultrasound-assisted extraction of phenolic compounds from grape marc: Influence of acoustic energy density and temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yang
Zhang, Zhihang
Sun, Da-Wen
coredata.dc:description: The effects of acoustic energy density (6.8–47.4W/L) and temperature (20–50°C) on the extraction yields of total phenolics and tartaric esters during ultrasound-assisted extraction from grape marc were investigated in this study. The ultrasound treatment was performed in a 25-kHz ultrasound bath system and the 50% aqueous ethanol was used as the solvent. The initial extraction rate and final extraction yield increased with the increase of acoustic energy density and temperature. The two site kinetic model was used to simulate the kinetics of extraction process and the diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was employed to determine the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolics in grape marc. Both models gave satisfactory quality of data fit. The diffusion process was divided into one fast stage and one slow stage and the diffusion coefficients in both stages were calculated. Within the current experimental range, the diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters for both diffusion stages increased with acoustic energy density. Meanwhile, the rise of temperature also resulted in the increase of diffusion coefficients of phenolics except the diffusion coefficient of total phenolics in the fast stage, the value of which being the highest at 40°C. Moreover, an empirical equation was suggested to correlate the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolics in grape marc with acoustic energy density and temperature. In addition, the performance comparison of ultrasound-assisted extraction and convention methods demonstrates that ultrasound is an effective and promising technology to extract bioactive substances from grape marc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Extraction
Grape marc
Phenolic compounds
Kinetic modeling
Effective diffusion coefficient

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetic modeling using two site kinetic model
Correlation between effective diffusion coefficient and experimental factors
Effect of acoustic energy density on extraction kinetics
Effect of temperature on extraction kinetics
Development of empirical correlations for describing effective diffusion coefficients
Performance comparison of ultrasound technology and conventional extraction technologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the experimental setup for ultrasound-assisted extraction. (1) Ultrasound generator; (2) ultrasound transducer; (3) water inlet; (4) water outlet and (5) 500-mL Erlenmeyer flask.

Evolution of extraction yields of total phenolics (A) and tartaric esters (B) as a function of time at different acoustic energy densities.
: 6.8W/L,
: 14.2W/L,
: 27.4W/L,
: 47.4W/L. The error bars show±standard deviation values.

Representative plot of ln(M ∞/(M ∞ − Mt )) against time at 27.4W/L and 40°C. Mt : the amount of total phenolics (mg/g) removed from raw materials at time t, M ∞ is the maximum amount of total phenolics (mg/g) extracted after infinite time.

Evolution of extraction yields of total phenolics (A) and tartaric esters (B) as a function of time at different temperatures.
: 20°C,
: 30°C,
: 40°C,
: 50°C. The error bars show±standard deviation values.

Arrhenius-type relationship. (A) The relationship between D e of total phenolics for slow diffusion stage and temperature; (B) the relationship between D e of tartaric esters for fast diffusion stage and temperature; (C) the relationship between D e of tartaric esters for slow diffusion stage and temperature.

Predicted versus experimental-determined values of effective diffusion coefficients of phenolics during ultrasound-assisted extraction from grape marcs. (A) D e of total phenolics during the fast diffusion stage; (B) D e of total phenolics during the slow diffusion stage; (C) D e of tartaric esters during the fast diffusion stage; (D) D e of tartaric esters during the slow diffusion stage.
Kinetic parameters obtained by fitting the two site kinetic model to the data of ultrasound-assisted extraction of grape marc at 40°C and 6.8–47.4W/L.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: F: fraction of solute released quickly.
1− F: fraction of solute related slowly.
k 1: The first-order rate constant representing the rapid released portion.
k 2: The first-order rate constant representing the slow released portion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effective diffusion coefficients of phenolic compounds during ultrasound-assisted extraction from grape marc particles at 40°C and 6.8–47.4W/L.
Kinetic parameters obtained by fitting the two site kinetic model to the data of ultrasound-assisted extraction of grape marc at 27.4W/L and 20–50°C.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: F: Fraction of solute released quickly.
1− F: Fraction of solute related slowly.
k 1: The first-order rate constant representing the rapid released portion.
k 2: The first-order rate constant representing the slow released portion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effective diffusion coefficients of phenolic compounds during ultrasound-assisted extraction of grape marc particles at 27.4W/L and 20–50°C.

head.ce:title: Kinetic modeling of ultrasound-assisted extraction of phenolic compounds from grape marc: Influence of acoustic energy density and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: FRCFT, School of Biosystems Engineering, University College Dublin, National University of Ireland, Agriculture and Food Science Centre, Belfield, Dublin 4, Ireland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effects of acoustic energy density (6.8–47.4W/L) and temperature (20–50°C) on the extraction yields of total phenolics and tartaric esters during ultrasound-assisted extraction from grape marc were investigated in this study. The ultrasound treatment was performed in a 25-kHz ultrasound bath system and the 50% aqueous ethanol was used as the solvent. The initial extraction rate and final extraction yield increased with the increase of acoustic energy density and temperature. The two site kinetic model was used to simulate the kinetics of extraction process and the diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was employed to determine the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolics in grape marc. Both models gave satisfactory quality of data fit. The diffusion process was divided into one fast stage and one slow stage and the diffusion coefficients in both stages were calculated. Within the current experimental range, the diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters for both diffusion stages increased with acoustic energy density. Meanwhile, the rise of temperature also resulted in the increase of diffusion coefficients of phenolics except the diffusion coefficient of total phenolics in the fast stage, the value of which being the highest at 40°C. Moreover, an empirical equation was suggested to correlate the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolics in grape marc with acoustic energy density and temperature. In addition, the performance comparison of ultrasound-assisted extraction and convention methods demonstrates that ultrasound is an effective and promising technology to extract bioactive substances from grape marc.

ce:section.ce:para: Grape marc, which is also known as grape pomace, is the solid remains of grape after pressing for juice in the juice and wine industry. It has been reported that million tons of grape marc are produced from the harvested grapes every year [1]. As one type of the agri-food industry by-products, grape marc is usually discarded as natural waste or utilized as compost or animal feed. On the other hand, grape marc is a good source of phenolic compounds, which are well-known for their health-beneficial properties, including free radical scavenging abilities and prevention of cancer, inflammations, cardiovascular diseases and aging-related disorders [2,3]. Therefore, the bioactive phenolic compounds recovered from grape marc can be potentially used in the food industry, thus generating economic benefits and reducing the environmental impact. Since the last decade, the interest of extracting phenolic compounds from grape-related by-products is growing continuously [1,4–14].

Phenolic compounds can be extracted from plant-based materials by various extraction technologies and solvents, depending on their distribution in the plant matrix and chemical properties. Among the current extraction technologies, ultrasound has been widely recognized as an effective, environment-friendly, safe and inexpensive technology, which is able to enhance various food processes [15–23]. When it is used to augment the solid -liquid extraction process, it can increase the extraction yield and shorten the extraction time [24–26]. The basis of ultrasound-assisted extraction is the ultrasonic cavitation, as well as the accompanied physical and chemical effects. Specifically, the propagation of ultrasonic waves in liquid medium can generate cavitation bubbles due to the pressure variation. The collapse of these bubbles can locally result in extremely high pressure and temperature, thus facilitating the destruction of material surface. In the meantime, microstreaming and microturbulence are generated in the liquid system, which provide a mechanical effect, allowing the penetration of solvent into raw materials [27]. According to our best knowledge, although ultrasound has been extensively utilized to extract bioactive compounds from plant-based materials, the influences of two important processing factors, namely acoustic energy density (AED) and temperature, on the extraction yields of phenolics from grape marc have not yet been elucidated.

From an engineering point of view, it is of paramount importance to use mathematical tool to model the extraction process and determine the kinetic parameters. Mathematical modeling [28–32] can facilitate the design, optimization and control of the processes under investigation, as well as provide useful information for scaling up the equipment. There are several theoretical, empirical and semi-empirical models, which can simulate the solid–liquid extraction of bioactive substances from natural materials, including pseudo first-order model, diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law, Weibull-type model, swelling/diffusion model, phenomenological model, two site kinetic model, etc. [8,33–35]. Furthermore, the diffusion of dissolved solutes within the solid matrix into the solvent is usually considered as the rate controlling step during the whole mass transfer process. The effective diffusion coefficient is an important parameter that can affect the diffusion process. In most cases, the diffusion coefficient of the solute during extraction can be calculated from the solution of Fick’s second law. The diffusion coefficients of phenolic compounds in grape-related by-products during solid–liquid extraction with agitation have been reported by several researchers [4,5,7]. However, the diffusion coefficients of phenolic compounds in grape-related by-products during ultrasound-assisted extraction have not been reported.

Therefore, the aims of this study were to investigate the effects of AED and temperature on the extraction yields of phenolic compounds (total phenolics and tartaric esters) from grape juice marc. Meanwhile, to better elucidate the mass transfer mechanism under ultrasound-assisted extraction, the extraction kinetic curves were modeled using two mass transfer models, namely the two site kinetic model and the diffusion model. In addition, an empirical equation was employed to correlate the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolic compounds with the processing factors, thus providing further information for scale-up of the sono-extractor based on the diffusion coefficient.

Fresh “Red Globe” table grapes grown in Peru were purchased from the local fruit market in Dublin. After pressing using a domestic juice extractor (RA-WJ1, Rachel Allen, Warrington, UK), the grape marc samples were obtained as the residue of the grapes, which consisted of grape seeds, skins and small amount of stalks. To make the grape marc crushable, these samples were dried in an oven at 45°C for 72h to about 10% moisture. After that, the grape marc samples were ground in a mortar manually and then sieved to select particle sizes between 0.425 and 0.600mm. The ground samples were sealed and stored at 4°C in darkness until use.

The ultrasound-assisted extraction was carried out in an ultrasonic bath system with a frequency of 25kHz (CQBF-1025, China Shipping Company, Shanghai, China). The experimental setup for the extraction process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . To be more specific, unidirectional ultrasonic waves were delivered from the transducers at the bottom of the stainless steel tank to the water inside the tank. The temperature of the water was maintained by a temperature circulator (LTD 6G, Grant, Cambridge, UK). Moreover, the nominal output power of the ultrasound generator could be adjusted from 0 to 300W.

An amount of 6.0g of aforementioned grape marc particles and 120mL of 50% aqueous ethanol solution were placed in a 500-mL Erlenmeyer flask. After sealing, this flask containing the mixture was immersed in the ultrasound bath and fixed well in the same position during sonication. The actual power dissipated in the flask was determined using the calorimetric method [36]. The acoustic energy density (AED) was then calculated by dividing the measured power by the volume of solution inside. Sonication was carried out continuously without any intervals. The extraction processes were performed in 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 80min, respectively. At the end of each extraction process, the crude extracts were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 3min and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45-μm filter. After that, the samples were stored in a dark room at 4°C until the analysis of phenolic composition.

The influences of AED and temperature on the extraction yields of phenolics were investigated by the “one-factor-at-a-time” strategy. Sonication was performed at 40°C at four different levels of AED (6.8, 14.2, 27.4 and 47.4W/L) to study the effect of AED. Furthermore, sonication was carried out at 27.4W/L at four different temperatures (20, 30, 40, 50°C) to explore the effect of temperature. All the treatments were carried out in triplicates.

The content of total phenolics in the extract was assayed using the Folin–Ciocalteu method and the result was expressed as g/L of gallic acid equivalents [37,38]. Meanwhile, the content of tartaric esters in the extract was measured by means of the method described by Cliff et al. [39]. Its content was expressed as mg/L of caffeic acid equivalents. The extraction yields in terms of total phenoclis and tartaric esters were then expressed as mg of gallic acid equivalents and mg of caffeic acid equivalents per gram of dried sample (mg/g), respectively. All the samples were analyzed in triplicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic modeling using two site kinetic model
ce:section.ce:para:
The two site kinetic model proposed by So and MacDonald [40], which assumes that there are two steps during the extraction process, namely desorption at a higher rate and desorption at a lower rate, was employed to model the extraction kinetics of phenolic compounds under sonication in this study. The equation is written as:
(1)
M
t
M
∞
= 1 -
Fe
-
k
1
t
- ( 1 - F )
e
-
k
2
t
where Mt is the amount of solute (mg/g) removed from raw materials at time t, M ∞ is the maximum amount of solute (mg/g) extracted after infinite time. F is the portion of solute released rapidly, (1− F) is the portion of solute released slowly, k 1 is the first-order rate constant representing the rapid released portion (min−1), k 2 is the first-order rate constant representing the slow released portion (min−1). The data fitting process was carried out in Matlab, R2010a (The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, USA).

To determine the effective diffusion coefficients of phenolics in grape marc during ultrasound-assisted extraction, the unsteady state diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was employed. Prior to utilization of the diffusion model to analyze the extraction process, several assumptions were made as follows:
(a) The geometry of the grape marc particles was considered as spherical. Since the particle sizes used varied between 0.425 and 0.600mm, the mean diameter of 0.513mm was used in this model.
(b) It is well-known that ultrasound processing can result in the convection in the bulk liquid medium due to the generation of cavitation bubbles, microstreaming, microturbulence, etc. [24]. Meanwhile, the volume of the extraction system in the study was 120mL, which was on a small scale. Therefore, the solvent in the extractor was assumed to be mixed perfectly and the external resistance to mass transfer was negligible.
(c) The effective diffusion coefficient of the extracted solute was considered to be constant.
(d) There were neither chemical reactions nor ultrasonic degradation of phenolics during the whole extraction process.

Based on the aforementioned assumptions, a one-dimensional non-steady state diffusion model derived from the Fick’s second law was used to describe the mass transfer of a solute from spherical particles [5]:
(2)
∂ C

∂ r

2

∂ r
where C is the solute concentration in the solid particle (kg/m3), r is the distance to the centre of spherical particle (m), D e is the solute effective diffusion coefficient (m2/s) and t is time (s).

The initial condition for solving the diffusion equation is shown below:
C ( r , 0 ) =
C
i
, 0 ≤ r ≤ R
where Ci is the initial solute concentration at the beginning of extraction (kg/m3) and R is the radius of grape marc particle (m).

In the meantime, the boundary conditions are described as follows:
∂ C ( 0 , t )
∂ r

t > 0
C ( R , t ) = 0
t > 0 ( assuming no solubility limitation )

Considering the initial and boundary conditions, the analytical solution of Eq. (2) is obtained [41]:
(3)

Therefore, the effective diffusion coefficient can be determined from the slop

of the straight line when plotting the logarithm of (M ∞/(M ∞ − Mt )) against time. Furthermore, it should be explained that although the intercept of this straight line given by Eq. (5) is ln(π 2/6), its value depends on the distribution of the solute, as well as the size and shape of particle in practice [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between effective diffusion coefficient and experimental factors
ce:section.ce:para:
After calculation of D e of phenolic compounds at different conditions, an empirical equation below was proposed to build the relationship between D e and the experimental factors under investigation, namely AED and temperature [44,45]:
(6)
D
e
=
a
0
( AED )
a
1
( T )
a
2
where a 0, a 1 and a 2 are regression coefficients. The data fitting process was carried out in Matlab, R2010a (The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, USA).

The performance of ultrasonic technology during extraction was compared with two conventional technologies, including maceration and extraction assisted by magnetic stirring. The maceration was performed in the same ultrasonic bath and this machine was switched off. The stirring-assisted extraction was aided by the use of a magnetic stirring device (RCT B, IKA Labortechnik, Staufen, Germany) at the speed of 700rpm. Furthermore, the ultrasound-assisted extraction was performed at 27.4W/L. The total phenolic yields during the aforementioned extraction processes at 20°C and 5, 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80min were compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acoustic energy density on extraction kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the amount of ultrasonic energy transmitted to the solvent plays an important role on the extraction efficiency and rate in most cases of ultrasound-assisted extraction [24]. AED was used to express the acoustic energy dissipated in the extraction system in this study. Theoretically, the acoustic energy can markedly affect the acoustic amplitude and the dynamics of cavitation bubbles [46], and subsequently influence the extraction progress. The variation of extraction yields during the ultrasound-assisted extraction process at different levels of AED is plotted in Fig. 2 . Generally, the extraction curves under sonication exhibit the same shape as those of conventional solid–liquid extraction of bioactive compounds, which is characterized by a high initial slope followed by an exponential shape [47]. The extraction yields of both total phenolics and tartaric esters in all treatments increased markedly in the first 10-min sonication and then increased slowly and steadily until reaching the equilibrium. The rapid increase of extraction yields in the initial stage of the process was mainly attributed to the simple washing of the extractable components located on the external surface of raw materials, as well as those within the particles with broken walls, while the slow increases of extraction yields was mainly controlled by the diffusion of dissolved solute within the intact particles. Considering that the washing process usually occurs instantaneously and it is even difficult to observe the washing period [48], the diffusion process was the rate-governing step.

Regarding the effect of AED on the evolution of extraction yields, it can be seen that the initial extraction rates of both responses (total phenolics and tartaric esters) increased along with the increase of the amount of acoustic energy dissipated in the system. Meanwhile, the total extraction yields also increased slightly with AED. The highest yield of 24.42mg/g for total phenolics was obtained after sonication for 80min at 47.4W/L. Similar results were obtained in the studies of ultrasound-assisted extractions of antioxidants from pomegranate peels and oil from pomegranate seeds, in which it was also found that the increase of output ultrasonic energy promoted the extraction process [49,50]. These results are reasonable since the increase of ultrasonic energy dissipated in the extractor can improve the mechanical and cavitation effects of ultrasound. Moreover, considering that both the initial extraction rate and the total extraction yield were influenced by AED, it could be deduced that ultrasound could affect both the washing and diffusion steps to enhance the extraction process.

The extraction kinetic curves at different levels of AED were modeled by means of the two site kinetic model in the form of Eq. (1). The kinetic parameters and the statistical analysis results are presented in Table 1 . As can be seen, the selected model fitted the experimental data well due to the high values of coefficient of determination (R 2) and the low values of root mean square error (RMSE). For all the investigated levels of AED, the fraction of phenolic compounds released fast was always much higher than that of phenolic compounds released slowly. Furthermore, the estimated rate constants for both fast and slow fractions increased steadily with AED. The rate constants for the fast extraction portion of total phenolics and tartaric esters varied from 0.0941 to 0.2622min−1 and 0.1012 to 0.3355min−1 in the AED range of 6.8–47.4W/L, respectively. Meanwhile, the rate constants for the slow extraction portion of total phenolics and tartaric esters in the same AED interval ranged from 0.0060 to 0.0290min−1 and 0.0141 to 0.0461min−1, respectively. These results demonstrated that the increase of AED during extraction could effectively improve the extraction rates of phenolics from grape marc.

To further investigate the mechanism of ultrasound-assisted extraction process, the diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was utilized to analyze the data and the effective diffusion coefficients at different levels of AED were calculated. After plotting the logarithm of (M ∞/(M ∞ − Mt )) against time, it was found that the points scattered along two intersecting straight lines, one with a relatively steep slope and another one with a smaller slope. A representative plot is depicted in Fig. 3 . This phenomenon also occurred in other studies relating to solid–liquid extraction [51–53]. These two lines correspond to the fast and slow stages of diffusion process within the particle and the intersection point is named transition point. This type of mass transfer behavior can be simulated by the following equation, which is re-arranged through transforming the solute concentration in the solvent to the mass transferred from the particle [52,53]:
(7)
M
∞
-
M
t
M
∞
=
6
π
2
f
1
exp
-
D
e - fast
π
2
t
R
2
+
f
2
exp
-
D
e - slow
π
2
t
R
2
where f 1 and f 2 represent the fractions of solute that are extracted with the diffusion coefficients D e-fast and D e-slow, respectively.

Although the two diffusion processes occur in parallel, all the terms in Eq. (7), except the second terms on the right side, are negligible in the later stage of extraction. As a result, the parameters of D e-slow and f 2 can be obtained from the shallow slope and intercept of the curve when plotting ln(M ∞/(M ∞ − Mt )) as a function of time t. On the other hand, D e-fast and f 1 can be determined assuming that the second exponential term is close to unity in the earlier stage of extraction [53].

In the representative plot of ln(M ∞/(M ∞ − Mt )) against time at 27.4W/L and 40°C (Fig. 3), the fast diffusion mainly denotes the unhindered diffusion of phenolic compounds from ruptured grape marc particles while the slow stage represents the hindered diffusion of phenolic compounds held in intact particles. On the other hand, it is noteworthy that the intercept of the line with a higher slope at t =0 is close to zero, indicating that there was no evident boundary between washing stage and unhindered diffusion stage during sonication in our case. Herodež et al. studied the extraction process under mechanical mixing and observed a positive intercept at t =0 due to the very high washing rate at the beginning of extraction [52]. The relatively low washing rate during sonication in the current study was probably ascribed to the lower convection intensity induced by ultrasound within the current experiment range, compared with mechanical agitation.

Moreover, the effective diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters at different levels of AED are summarized in Table 2 . The values of D e-fast of both total phenolics and tartaric esters increased with the increase of AED from 6.8 to 47.4W/L by a factor of 3.96 for total phenolics and that of 3.59 for tartaric esters, which was consistent with the evolution of first-order rate constants in the two site kinetic model. Under sonication at 47.4W/L, the D e-fast values of total phenolics and tartaric esters were 5.82×10−11 and 8.21×10−11 m2/s, respectively. The high value of D e-fast corresponded to the rapid initial mass transfer of solute toward the external surface due to the mechanical damage of the particle surface during milling and the simply washing effect. Similarly, D e-slow of both total phenolics and tartaric esters also increased as AED increased in the current experimental range. The D e-slow of total phenolics varied with AED from 3.81×10−12 to 4.98×10−12 m2/s while the corresponding values of tartaric esters increased from 4.76×10−12 to 6.44×10−12 m2/s. Regarding other techniques used for extraction of phenolics from grape by-products, Pinelo et al. reported an effective diffusion coefficient value of 105.49×10−14 m2/s of total phenolics during the continuous extraction of antioxidant from the residue of white Garnacha grapes at 50°C [4]. Moreover, the study of Bucić-Kojić et al. revealed that the effective diffusivity of total phenolics was 0.42×10−12 m2/s when grape seeds were extracted with 50% ethanol under agitation at 40°C [54]. Although the diffusivity of phenolics depends on both the extraction conditions and the nature of raw materials, it can still be concluded that phenolics can exhibit a considerably fast diffusion within the grape by-product particles under sonication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on extraction kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the extraction kinetics under sonication is illustrated in Fig. 4 . Similar to the effect of AED on the extraction kinetics, the extraction yields increased with increasing temperature from 20 to 50°C. After 80min of extraction, the extraction yields of total phenolics and tartaric esters at 20°C were 20.08 and 1.20mg/g while the corresponding yields increased to 25.38 and 1.57mg/g at 50°C. The degradation of phenolic compounds in the temperature range investigated was not observed. Generally, the rising of temperature can accelerate the softening and swelling of the raw materials, increase the solubility of extracted compounds and decrease the viscosity of solvent [24]. As a result, the mass transfer of phenolics from grape marc particles was improved when the temperature was increased gradually. Similar results were obtained when investigating the extraction kinetics of resinoid from the aerial parts of white lady’s bedstraw under sonication from 23 to 40°C [35]. On the other hand, the study of Goula found that the increase of temperature from 20 to 80°C resulted in a decrease in extraction yield when using ultrasound to extract pomegranate seed oil [50]. Despite the fact that high temperature in an appropriate range can facilitate ultrasound-assisted extraction, its negative effect on ultrasonic cavitation intensity should not be neglected. The rising of extraction temperature can result in the increase of vapor pressure and decrease of surface tension, thus decreasing the energies released during the collapse of cavitation bubbles [55].

On the other hand, although the increase in temperature generally increased the first-order rate constants of extraction in the two site kinetic model, the rate constant for the slow released portion of total phenolics at 50°C and that for the fast released portion of tartaric esters at 30°C were lower than the corresponding values at 40 and 20°C, respectively (Table 3 ). As for the effective diffusion coefficients at different temperatures (Table 4 ), both values of D e-fast and D e-slow of tartaric esters and values of D e-slow of total phenolics increased when the temperature was increased. The magnitude of D e-slow increased from 3.94×10−12 to 5.09×10−12 m2/s for total phenolics and 3.34×10−12 to 6.26×10−12 m2/s for tartaric esters when the temperature rose from 20 to 50°C. The value of D e-fast of tartaric esters also increased by a factor of 2.05 when the temperature reached 50°C. All these results are consistent with the well-established Einstein equation, which proposes that a significant increase of temperature can lead to the increase of diffusivity [56]. On the other hand, the highest D e-fast of 4.92×10−11 m2/s for total phenolics was obtained at 40°C. With a further rise of temperature to 50°C, its value decreased to 4.51×10−11 m2/s. Hojnik et al. and Bucić-Kojić et al. also reported that phenolic compounds exhibited a higher effective diffusivity at a lower temperature during extraction with mechanical agitation [53,54].

To further investigate the effect of temperature on the diffusion coefficient of phenolics during sonication, the data of D e-fast and D e-slow for tartaric esters and D e-slow for total phenolics were fitted to a linearized Arrhenius type equation shown below:
(8)
lnD
e
=
lnD
0
-
E
a
R
g
T
abs
where D e is the effective diffusion coefficient, D 0 is the initial diffusion coefficient, E a is the energy of activation for diffusion, R g is the universal gas constant (8.314J/molK) and T abs is the absolute temperature. The activation energy can be estimated from the slope of the plot of ln D e against reciprocal absolute temperature (Fig. 5 ). More specifically, the activation energies for tartaric esters during the fast diffusion and slow diffusion stages were 19.9 and 16.5kJ/mol. In the meantime, the activation energy for total phenolics during the slow diffusion stage was 7.0kJ/mol. Cacace and Mazza reported the activation energy for total phenolics ranging from 70–97kJ/mol during the extraction from milled berries under agitation, which was also calculated from the diffusion coefficient [56]. Moreover, the activation energy for lignans, calculated from the diffusion coefficient during pressurized low polarity water extraction from flaxseed meal, was 51–56kJ/mol [33]. As can be seen, the activation energy for phenolics during ultrasound treatment was lower than that for phenolics under mechanical agitation and for lignans under pressurized extraction, indicating a lower energy barrier to initiate diffusion. It has been reported that the activation energy required during extraction process can be reduced by thermal energies, thus promoting the disruption of the strong solute-matrix and solute–solute interactions [57]. Hereby, it was deduced that the collapse of cavitation bubbles could provide the thermal energies for the decline of activation energy during extraction. On the other hand, the activation energy for total phenolics during the fast diffusion stage of extraction was not calculated, since the variation of diffusion coefficient with temperature was not in accordance to the Arrhenius relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of empirical correlations for describing effective diffusion coefficients
ce:section.ce:para: According to the aforementioned results, it was found that the effective diffusion coefficient of phenolics at both fast and slow diffusion stages varied along with AED and temperature during extraction. In the next step, the empirical equation (Eq. (6)) was used to correlate the effective diffusion coefficients with the processing factors. Normally, the development of empirical correlations for prediction of interest is the standard approach for scaling up the sono-reactors, since it is quite difficult to model the physical phenomena generated by ultrasound [45].

The proposed correlations for estimating the effective diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters are shown as follows:
(9)
D
e - fast
( TP ) = 8.913 ×
10
- 30
×
AED
0.6927
×

abs
7.087
(10)
D
e - slow
( TP ) = 5.525 ×
10
- 19
×

0.1459
×
T
abs
2.701
(11)

e - fast
( TP ) = 2.710 ×
10
- 33
×

0.5723
×
T

8.606
(12)
D
e - slow
( TP ) = 5.180 ×
10
- 29

0.1389
×
T
abs
6.743
where AED is the acoustic energy density (W/L), T abs is the absolute temperature (K), TP denotes total phenolics, TE represents tartaric esters. The predicted values of D e (m2/s) using Eqs. (9)–(12) show satisfactory agreement with the values determined from the experimental data, since either the slopes of each regression line or the R 2 coefficients are close to 1 (Fig. 6 ). Furthermore, it should be pointed out that the above correlations are mainly valid in the experimental range under investigation, namely AED from 6.8–47.4W/L and temperature from 20–50°C. In general, the obtained empirical correlations provide a simple and effective approach for modeling the sono-extractor, which can be used for scaling-up and designing the ultrasonic equipment used for extraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison of ultrasound technology and conventional extraction technologies
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the advantage of ultrasound technology, its extraction performance was compared with two conventional extraction methods. As shown in Fig. 7 , magnetic stirring was more effective than ultrasound to extract phenolic compounds from grape marc particles in the first 10min of extraction. After that, the total phenolic yield under sonication was higher than that under magnetic stirring. This result implied that the mechanical effect played a more important role in the rapid increase of extraction yield in the early stage of extraction. Meanwhile, the caviation effect induced by ultrasound was a better contributor to the increase of extraction yield in the diffusion stage, especially the slow diffusion stage. Furthermore, the extraction yield of total phenolics obtained from maceration was always the lowest during the 80min of extraction. All these results clearly demonstrate that ultrasound is a competitive and effective extraction technology.

This is the first study investigating the influences of two processing factors, namely AED and temperature, on the extraction yields of total phenolics and tartaric esters during ultrasound-assisted extraction from grape marc. The results indicated that both the initial extraction rate and final extraction yield increased with the increasing of AED and temperature. To further elucidate the mass transfer phenomena during sonication, the two site kinetic model and the diffusion model derived from the Fick’s second law were utilized. Both models were able to fit the experimental data with satisfactory accuracy. In the meantime, two parallel diffusion processes, including one fast stage and one slow stage, were taken into account in the diffusion model and the effective diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters in both stages were determined. Generally, the increase of AED and temperature could result in the increase of diffusion coefficients (with the diffusion coefficient of total phenolics in the fast stage being the exception). The diffusion coefficient of total phenolics in the fast diffusion stage during sonication at 40°C was higher than that at 50°C when the level of AED was constant. Moreover, the calculated effective diffusion coefficients of total phenolics and tartaric esters were correlated well with AED and temperature in the experimental range under investigation. In addition, ultrasound technology proved to be more effective than conventional technologies during extraction from grape marc through performance comparison. Overall, all these results can provide useful information for designing, optimizing and scaling up the ultrasound-assisted extraction process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Yang Tao thanks the Chinese Scholarship Council for supporting his PhD study (under UCD-CSC funding programme).
